answer:
**The Yama Smṛti (also spelled Yama Smṛti) in this corpus does not prescribe a *specific* procedure for purification after contact with a corpse.**[^1]

The text focuses primarily on penances (*vrata*, *kṛcchra*, *cāndrāyaṇa*, etc.) for various sins (e.g., eating forbidden food, intercourse with prohibited persons or animals, killing, etc.), general rules for ritual purity (e.g., water as a purifier), and punishments involving corpses (e.g., smearing a punished person with corpse ashes or hair as a form of degradation).[^2][^3]

A general rule appears for contact with *any* impure substance (*amedhya*):  
> A twice-born who is touched by an impure substance, by night or by day, / should immediately bathe and touch fire; and at the two twilights, he is always pure.[^4]

Corpse contact is not singled out here or elsewhere (unlike in other smṛtis like Manu Smṛti or Viṣṇu Smṛti, which address it explicitly). No terms like *śava* (corpse), *preta* (departed spirit), or death-related *aśauca* (impurity) appear in purification contexts.[^5]

[^1]: Full chapters: Yama Smṛti, ch. 1 ([/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/01/translation/](#)) and ch. 2 ([/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/02/translation/](#)). The work has only these two chapters.
[^2]: Yama Smṛti, ch. 1, [p-65c739](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/01/translation/#p-65c739).
[^3]: Yama Smṛti, ch. 2, [p-c0845b](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/02/translation/#p-c0845b) (punishment: "With his body smeared with ashes and fumigated with the hair and bones of a corpse...").
[^4]: Yama Smṛti, ch. 1, [p-65c739](/works/unknown-yama-smrti-552f8d/01/translation/#p-65c739).
[^5]: Confirmed via full-text search across the work for corpse/death/purification terms; no matches in relevant contexts.
